message.content:
The salt spray had long since bleached Elias’s skin to the color of old parchment, and the rhythm of the waves had become the only clock he trusted. For twenty years, he had tended the Needle, a jagged spire of granite jutting out from the edge of the Blackwood Coast. He was a man of silence, content to let the gulls scream and the wind howl while he polished the great Fresnel lens until it caught the moonlight like a diamond.

It was a Tuesday, or perhaps a Wednesday; the days blurred into a monochrome loop of gray sea and white foam, when the bottle washed up.

It wasn’t like the ones tourists threw in from the pier miles away. This bottle was thick, green glass, heavy with age, and sealed with wax that had turned the color of dried blood. It had wedged itself into a crevice of the rocks below the lighthouse, shielded from the tide by a shelf of barnacle-encrusted stone.

Elias climbed down, his knees popping in protest, and pried it free. The glass was cold, unnaturally so. He carried it up to the lantern room, placing it on the iron railing beside the logbook. He didn’t intend to open it. He knew better than to disturb the sea’s offerings. But the wind was howling that night, a mournful wail that seemed to vibrate in his teeth, and curiosity, that old, persistent itch, finally won out.

He used his pocket knife to chip away the wax. The cork gave way with a wet pop. Inside was a single sheet of paper, folded into a tight square, preserved by the oil and the seal.

Elias unfolded it with trembling fingers. The handwriting was elegant, looping, and unmistakably modern.

*“If you are reading this, the storm has passed
